description: This transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image displays the dislocation substructure in an Fe–20% Al alloy (atomic percent) following deformation to 2–3% strain. The micrograph, captured with the electron beam parallel to the [0 0 1] zone axis and using a g = 1 1 0 imaging vector (indicated by the arrow), reveals a complex arrangement of dislocations characteristic of disordered or short-range ordered bcc solid solutions. The field of view shows straight, near-screw dislocations—some appearing loosely paired—together with numerous edge dipole segments and small prismatic loops. These features are identified as single dislocations with Burgers vector (1/2)[1 1 1] gliding through the matrix, where the straight screw segments result from strong Peierls forces, while the dipoles and loops trail behind as debris from cross-slip or local pinning events. The scale bar indicates 0.2 μm, confirming the nanoscale resolution of these defects. This figure provides direct evidence that low-Al-content Fe–Al alloys deform via single dislocation motion rather than superdislocation pairs, supporting the mechanistic understanding of work hardening in these materials where dislocation multiplication and dipole hardening dominate the strain-hardening behavior.
